# Catalytic Reaction Engineering

![Sustainability Directory](https://sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/04/Sustainability-Directory-Logo-Icon.svg)

## Table of Contents

## Catalytic Reaction Engineering → Fashion

Catalytic Reaction Engineering represents a foundational discipline within chemical engineering, primarily concerned with the design, analysis, and operation of chemical reactors where catalytic reactions occur. At its core, this field seeks to understand how catalysts influence reaction rates and selectivities, and how these insights can be leveraged to build efficient and sustainable chemical processes. Imagine a world where desired chemical transformations happen rapidly, under mild conditions, and produce minimal unwanted byproducts; this is the realm Catalytic Reaction Engineering strives to create. It bridges the gap between fundamental chemistry → understanding molecular interactions and reaction mechanisms → and large-scale industrial implementation, ensuring that laboratory discoveries translate into economically viable and environmentally sound production methods.

The fundamental principles governing this field revolve around chemical kinetics, thermodynamics, and transport phenomena. Kinetics dictates the speed of a reaction, thermodynamics defines its feasibility and equilibrium limits, while transport phenomena (mass and heat transfer) describe how reactants reach the catalyst surface and how products leave, alongside managing [energy Meaning → The Energy domain delves into the transition from fossil fuels to renewable sources and the future of power generation.](https://energy.sustainability-directory.com/)  flows within the reactor.

Catalysts, whether homogeneous (in the same phase as reactants) or heterogeneous (in a different phase), function by lowering the activation [energy Meaning → The Energy domain delves into the transition from fossil fuels to renewable sources and the future of power generation.](https://energy.sustainability-directory.com/)  of a reaction pathway, effectively providing an alternative route that requires less energy input. This ability to accelerate reactions is paramount for industrial processes, enabling higher throughputs and lower operating costs compared to non-catalytic routes.

![Discarded textile and paper waste litter pollutes dark waters in a stark depiction of environmental contamination. The image highlights anthropogenic pollution and the detrimental environmental impact of unsustainable waste management practices. Textile and paper refuse, along with organic debris are shown submerged, reflecting ecosystem pollution and ecological imbalance caused by resource depletion.](https://sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/discarded-textile-and-paper-waste-litter-submerged-in-dark-waters-illustrating.jpg "Discarded Textile and Paper Waste Litter Submerged in Dark Waters Illustrating Environmental Contamination and Anthropogenic Pollution")

### Core Concepts in Catalysis

Understanding the catalyst itself is central to the engineering challenge. Catalysts are not consumed in the overall reaction, though they may undergo temporary chemical changes. Their activity, selectivity, and stability are critical performance metrics.

These catalyst properties are intrinsically linked to their physical and chemical characteristics, including surface area, pore structure, active site composition, and morphology. Engineering the catalyst involves tailoring these properties to match the specific reaction requirements.

![A stepped metallic cylinder exemplifies reclaimed materials demonstrating embedded energy concerns in engineering. Comprising telescoping sections and material scarcity and lifecycle, a visual study illustrates the embodied carbon footprint impact. The image resonates industrial ecology and sustainable development goals in closed loop systems.](https://sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/reclaimed-materials-a-depiction-of-telescoping-mechanical-component-revealing.jpg "Reclaimed Materials a Depiction of Telescoping Mechanical Component Revealing Embedded Embodied Energy in Resource Depletion Framework")

### Reactor Types and Considerations

Selecting and designing the appropriate reactor vessel is equally vital. The choice depends on the reaction kinetics, catalyst type, operating conditions (temperature, pressure), and desired production scale.

Common reactor configurations include:

Reactor design requires balancing reaction rate with transport limitations. In heterogeneous catalysis, reactants must diffuse from the bulk fluid to the catalyst surface, into the catalyst pores, react at the active site, and then products must diffuse back out. These mass transfer steps can become rate-limiting, slowing down the overall process despite a highly active catalyst. Similarly, managing the heat generated or consumed by the reaction is paramount to maintaining optimal temperature profiles and preventing catalyst damage or runaway reactions.

Catalytic Reaction Engineering translates molecular insights into industrial reality, enabling efficient chemical transformations.

Consider the synthesis of ammonia via the Haber-Bosch process, a cornerstone of fertilizer production. This reaction requires high pressures and temperatures, yet the iron-based catalyst dramatically lowers the energy barrier compared to the non-catalyzed reaction. Engineers designing the Haber-Bosch reactor must account for the catalyst’s activity and stability under these harsh conditions, manage the significant heat released, and ensure efficient contact between the gas phase reactants and the solid catalyst particles within the packed bed.

The success of this process, despite its energy intensity, highlights the [power Meaning → Power is the rate at which energy is generated and consumed, with a focus on transitioning to clean and renewable sources.](https://energy.sustainability-directory.com/)  of catalysis in enabling large-scale chemical synthesis that would otherwise be impractical.

Basic Reactor Types and Characteristics

| Reactor Type | Catalyst Phase | Typical Application | Key Engineering Challenge |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Packed Bed Reactor (PBR) | Solid (Heterogeneous) | Gas-phase reactions (e.g., ammonia synthesis, sulfuric acid production) | Pressure drop, heat management |
| Fluidized Bed Reactor (FBR) | Solid (Heterogeneous) | Reactions with high heat release/consumption (e.g., catalytic cracking) | Catalyst attrition, backmixing |
| Stirred Tank Reactor (STR) | Homogeneous or Solid (Heterogeneous) | Liquid-phase reactions (e.g., polymerization, hydrogenation) | Mixing efficiency, mass transfer (for heterogeneous) |

The interplay between catalyst properties, reactor configuration, and operating conditions forms the fundamental puzzle pieces engineers manipulate to optimize catalytic processes. Mastering these basics provides the necessary foundation for tackling more complex challenges in sustainable chemical manufacturing.

![A modern recycled polymer resin composite padlock glistens with water droplets demonstrating sustainable engineering design within a circular economy. The image highlights material recovery and responsible production principles supporting environmental stewardship and waste minimization. Its design illustrates considerations towards carbon footprint reduction and innovative reuse, integral to sustainability.](https://sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/sustainable-material-engineering-a-wet-recycled-polymer-resin-composite-padlock.jpg "Sustainable Material Engineering a Wet Recycled Polymer Resin Composite Padlock Illustrating Circular Economy Principles")

## Catalytic Reaction Engineering → Pollution

To grasp the significance of **Catalytic Reaction Engineering**, one should start with its fundamental **definition** → it is the discipline concerned with the design, analysis, and optimization of chemical reactors where catalytic reactions occur. In simpler **terms**, it’s about making chemical processes faster, more efficient, and cleaner by using substances called catalysts. These catalysts, by their very **nature**, participate in a reaction mechanism, speeding it up without being consumed themselves. This distinction is **key**; catalysts are not reactants in the stoichiometric sense, but rather facilitators of chemical transformations.

Their role is to lower the activation [energy Meaning → The Energy domain delves into the transition from fossil fuels to renewable sources and the future of power generation.](https://energy.sustainability-directory.com/)  barrier, which is the energy hurdle that molecules must overcome to react. This **explanation** immediately highlights the practical **significance** of catalysis, especially within the realm of sustainability, where efficiency and minimizing waste are paramount.

Catalytic Reaction Engineering fundamentally aims to enhance the efficiency and sustainability of chemical processes by leveraging the power of catalysts.

The **description** of a catalytic reaction engineering system always begins with the **catalyst** itself. Catalysts come in diverse forms, ranging from solid materials like zeolites and metal oxides to soluble complexes and enzymes. The choice of catalyst is dictated by the specific reaction and desired outcome. For instance, in the petroleum industry, solid catalysts are ubiquitously employed for cracking large hydrocarbon molecules into smaller, more valuable ones, a process crucial for gasoline production.

In contrast, homogeneous catalysts, dissolved in the reaction medium, find applications in fine chemical synthesis where precise control over selectivity is needed. This **delineation** between catalyst types is not merely academic; it directly dictates [reactor design Meaning → Reactor Design, within the realm of sustainability, denotes the strategic engineering and optimization of systems where controlled chemical or biological transformations occur.](https://pollution.sustainability-directory.com/area/reactor-design/)  and operating conditions.

![An abstract study in sustainable fluid dynamics, manifesting as a transparent form indicative of bio-based polymer innovation and circularity in material engineering. The interplay of light suggests clean technology applications, promoting resource efficiency and ecological balance. This image embodies environmentally conscious design and green chemistry principles, advocating for renewable resources and responsible manufacturing within a sustainable framework.](https://sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/visualization-of-sustainable-fluid-dynamics-in-bio-based-polymer-engineering.jpg "Visualization of Sustainable Fluid Dynamics in Bio-Based Polymer Engineering for Eco-Conscious Materiality and Circularity")

### Basic Principles of Catalysis

Understanding **catalysis** requires appreciating the underlying principles. **Heterogeneous catalysis**, where the catalyst is in a different phase from the reactants (typically a solid catalyst and gaseous or liquid reactants), is governed by a series of steps → reactant adsorption onto the catalyst surface, surface reaction, and product desorption. Each step presents opportunities for engineering intervention to enhance overall reaction rates. **Homogeneous catalysis**, on the other hand, operates in a single phase.

Its mechanisms often involve intricate coordination chemistry, where the catalyst forms intermediate complexes with reactants, facilitating bond breaking and formation in a controlled manner. The **import** of understanding these mechanistic differences lies in the tailored approaches needed for reactor design and optimization in each case.

Reactor design in **Catalytic Reaction Engineering** is not a one-size-fits-all endeavor. The choice of reactor type is influenced by factors such as the phase of reactants and products, the type of catalyst, and the desired production scale. Common reactor configurations include fixed-bed reactors, fluidized-bed reactors, and slurry reactors. Fixed-bed reactors, packed with solid catalyst particles, are widely used in large-scale petrochemical processes due to their simplicity and robustness.

Fluidized-bed reactors offer excellent temperature control and are suitable for reactions involving fine catalyst particles. Slurry reactors are employed for reactions involving solid catalysts dispersed in a liquid phase. This **specification** of reactor types reveals the practical considerations that engineers must address to implement [catalytic processes Meaning → Processes using substances (catalysts) to speed up reactions without being consumed, crucial for efficiency and sustainability.](https://pollution.sustainability-directory.com/term/catalytic-processes/)  effectively.

Kinetics and reactor modeling are integral to **Catalytic Reaction Engineering**. **Kinetic studies** are conducted to determine the rate of reaction and how it varies with temperature, pressure, and reactant concentrations. This data is then used to develop mathematical models that describe reactor performance. These models are essential tools for reactor design, optimization, and scale-up.

**Elucidation** of reaction kinetics allows engineers to predict reactor behavior under different operating conditions and to identify potential bottlenecks that limit performance. Furthermore, understanding mass and heat transfer phenomena within the reactor is equally vital. In many catalytic reactors, especially those employing solid catalysts, reaction rates can be limited by the transport of reactants to the catalyst surface or the removal of products. Addressing these transport limitations is a key aspect of reactor design and optimization, impacting the overall **meaning** of efficiency and productivity.

![A symbolic agave embodies algal biorefining for sustainable biofuel applications. Reflecting on carbon sequestration for bioeconomy via closed loop processes, its geometry mirrors the bio catalytic resource optimization of industrial ecology and integrated waste management. The image echoes sustainability and the zero waste ambition through the cultured biomass via photosynthetic conversion for carbon footprint minimization.](https://sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/photobioreactor-aesthetic-a-botanical-biorefining-depiction-cultured-microalgae.jpg "Photobioreactor Aesthetic a Botanical Biorefining Depiction Cultured Microalgae Biomass and Resource Efficiency Sustainability.")

## Catalytic Reaction Engineering → Energy

To grasp the **definition** of **Catalytic Reaction Engineering**, it’s helpful to first understand its components separately. At its heart, it is a field rooted in chemical engineering, specifically focused on chemical reactions that are accelerated or made more efficient through the use of **catalysts**. Imagine a chemical reaction as a journey from point A (reactants) to point B (products). This journey often requires a certain amount of energy to overcome a barrier, like pushing a ball uphill.

**Catalysts** act like tunnels through this hill, lowering the energy barrier and making the journey → the reaction → much faster and easier. This is the basic **explanation** of catalysis.

**Catalytic Reaction Engineering** then takes this concept and applies engineering principles to it. It’s not just about knowing catalysts speed up reactions, but understanding **how** they do it, **what** factors influence their effectiveness, and most importantly, **how to design and operate reactors** that utilize catalysts optimally for desired chemical transformations. The **description** therefore, extends beyond simple chemistry; it becomes a practical discipline concerned with reactor design, process optimization, and scale-up of catalytic processes.

Catalytic Reaction Engineering, in its simplest form, is the application of engineering principles to chemical reactions involving catalysts to optimize processes.

Consider a common **example** → the catalytic converter in a car. Its primary **intention** is to reduce harmful emissions like nitrogen oxides (NOx), carbon monoxide (CO), and unburnt hydrocarbons from exhaust gases. Inside the converter, precious metals like platinum, palladium, and rhodium act as catalysts. Without these catalysts, the reactions to convert these pollutants into less harmful substances like nitrogen, carbon dioxide, and water would be too slow to be effective in a moving vehicle.

The **significance** here is immense → catalytic converters drastically reduce air [pollution Meaning → The Pollution domain investigates the sources, impacts, and remediation of contaminants in our air, water, and soil.](https://pollution.sustainability-directory.com/)  in urban environments, demonstrating the real-world **import** of **Catalytic Reaction Engineering**. This simple **statement** exemplifies how catalytic reaction engineering tackles real-world problems related to energy and [sustainability Meaning → Sustainability is the capacity for human civilization to coexist with the Earth’s biosphere in a state of equilibrium.](https://sustainability-directory.com/)  by cleaning up exhaust.

In the context of **energy in sustainability**, the **meaning** of **Catalytic Reaction Engineering** deepens. It’s not just about faster reactions, but about **more efficient** and **cleaner** energy production and utilization. Traditional energy processes, such as burning fossil fuels, often involve inefficient and polluting reactions. **Catalytic Reaction Engineering** offers solutions to improve these processes or develop entirely new, sustainable energy pathways.

For instance, catalysts are crucial in refining crude oil into gasoline and other fuels, but also in developing biofuels from renewable resources like biomass. The **elucidation** here is that by optimizing catalytic processes, we can reduce energy [consumption Meaning → Consumption refers to the use of goods and services, with a sustainable focus on mindful, responsible, and circular patterns.](https://eco.sustainability-directory.com/)  in refining, make better use of raw materials, and transition towards cleaner energy sources.

This aligns directly with sustainability goals by reducing reliance on fossil fuels and minimizing environmental impact. The **connotation** of **Catalytic Reaction Engineering** in sustainability becomes strongly associated with environmental responsibility and resource efficiency.

To further clarify, let’s break down the core concepts within **Catalytic Reaction Engineering** →

In essence, **Catalytic Reaction Engineering** is a holistic discipline that bridges fundamental chemistry and engineering practice. It provides the tools and knowledge to harness the [power Meaning → Power is the rate at which energy is generated and consumed, with a focus on transitioning to clean and renewable sources.](https://energy.sustainability-directory.com/)  of catalysis for a wide range of applications, particularly within the critically important domain of energy and sustainability. Its **substance** lies in enabling chemical processes that are not only faster and more efficient but also cleaner and more resource-effective, which is paramount for a sustainable future.

The **denotation** therefore emphasizes its practical, problem-solving nature applied to chemical transformations using catalysts. The **essence** of this field is about making chemical reactions work better for humanity and the planet.

Moving beyond the basics, it’s important to recognize that **Catalytic Reaction Engineering** is not a static field. It’s constantly evolving, driven by the ever-increasing demands for sustainable technologies and the continuous discovery of new catalytic materials and reaction pathways. The **clarification** needed now moves towards its dynamic nature, adapting to new scientific discoveries and global challenges.

The ongoing advancements in Catalytic Reaction Engineering are central to achieving significant progress in sustainable energy and chemical processes.

Publisher Summary
-----------------

Catalytic reaction engineering is a scientific discipline which bridges the gap between the fundamentals of catalysis and its industrial application. Starting from insight into reaction mechanisms provided by catalytic chemists and surface scientists, the rate equations are developed, which allow a quantitative description of the effects of the reaction conditions on reaction rates and on selectivities for desired products. The study of intrinsic reaction kinetics, that is, those determined solely by chemical events, belongs to the core of catalytic reaction engineering. Very close to it lays the study of the interaction between physical transport and chemical reaction. Such interactions can have pronounced effects on the rates and selectivities obtained in industrial reactors. They have to be accounted for explicitly when scaling up from laboratory to industrial dimensions. The major reactor categories discussed in the chapter includes: batch reactor, plug flow reactor, continuous flow stirred tank reactor (CSTR), and nonisothermal reactors. The chapter presents an analysis of the effect of the finite rate at which reaction components and/or heat are supplied to or removed from the locus of reaction, that is, the catalytic site.

*   ### [Kinetics of cinnamaldehyde hydrogenation-concentration dependent selectivity](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0920586100006349)

2001, Catalysis Today  Show abstract For the isothermal hydrogenation of cinnamaldehyde over Pt/C/monolithic catalysts three kinetic models have been evaluated, each model based on different sets of assumptions. A single-site and a two-site model with the surface reactions as rate-controlling steps were unable to describe the experimental data satisfactorily, while a single-site model with adsorption, surface reactions, and desorption all rate controlling could describe the experimental data adequately. This model includes apparent direct reaction steps from reactant to various end-products, due to the competition between desorption and subsequent conversion of intermediates at the catalyst surface. The selectivity to the unsaturated alcohol increases with increasing reactant concentration, which is ascribed to a change in adsorption mode, possibly due to some assembling phenomena of the reactant at the catalyst surface.

SUMMARY:
**Abstract**: A catalyst is a substance which can increase the rate of a chemical reaction. k - reaction rate constant, 1/s  From the chemical kinetics point of view, this is represented as a dynamic balance, where the original reactants _A_, _B_, _C_, … are being re-created in a reverse reaction between _R_, _S_, …. The net reaction rate equals zero at equilibrium, _r 0 = 0_, thus Fortunately, reaction rates may be increased by the use of catalysts. FULL CONTENT:
Catalyst Fundamentals

===============

[DieselNet Technology Guide](https://dieselnet.com/tg) » [Emission Control Catalysts](https://dieselnet.com/tech/catalysts.php)

[DieselNet](https://dieselnet.com/) | Copyright © ECOpoint Inc. | Revision 2023.09

Catalyst Fundamentals
=====================

W. Addy Majewski

[](https://dieselnet.com/login?url=/tech/cat_fund.php "Log in")This is a preview of the paper, limited to some initial content. Full access requires DieselNet [subscription](https://dieselnet.com/subscribe.html).

**[Please log in](https://dieselnet.com/login?url=/tech/cat_fund.php)** to view the complete version of this paper.

**Abstract**: A catalyst is a substance which can increase the rate of a chemical reaction. Heterogeneous catalysts supported on high surface area porous oxides are used in emission control applications. The overall catalytic conversion in a heterogeneous catalyst is composed of several sub-processes which involve chemical reaction, bulk mass transfer and pore diffusion. Catalytic processes controlled by reaction kinetics can be modeled using the Arrhenius equation. Catalytic conversions in the mass transfer controlled region can be estimated using mass transfer correlations developed for monolithic catalyst supports. Even though catalysts are not used in the reaction, they undergo gradual deterioration due to thermal deactivation and poisoning. 

*   [Chemical Reaction Kinetics](https://dieselnet.com/tech/cat_fund.php#kinetics)
*   [Definitions and Basic Terms](https://dieselnet.com/tech/cat_fund.php#defs)
*   [Conversion Rate in Heterogeneous Catalyst](https://dieselnet.com/tech/cat_fund.php#rate)
*   [Catalyst Deactivation](https://dieselnet.com/tech/cat_fund.php#deactiv)

Chemical Reaction Kinetics
--------------------------

A chemical reaction that results from the simultaneous combination of _a_ molecules of _A_, _b_ molecules of _B_, and _c_ molecules of _C_ … can be represented by the following equation:

aA + bB + cC … ↔ rR + sS + …(1)

The reaction rate for the forward reaction may be written as

r = k·C A a·C B b·C C c…(2)

where: 

r - rate of reaction, kmole/(m 3·s) 

k - reaction rate constant, 1/s 

C i - concentration of reactant _i_, kmole/m 3.

Equation (2) defines the reaction rate constant (k) which, under ideal conditions, depends only on temperature and the nature of the reaction. Another important term is the _order of the reaction_, defined as the sum of the exponents _a_, _b_, _c_ … of Equation (1). This sum equals to 1 for a first-order reaction, 2 for a second-order reaction, etc.

Some chemical reactions may not proceed completely to the end, i.e., to the right side of Equation (1). Rather, a _thermodynamic equilibrium_ may be established where certain concentrations of reactants _A_, _B_, _C_, … will co-exist in the system with certain concentrations of products _R_, _S_, …. From the chemical kinetics point of view, this is represented as a dynamic balance, where the original reactants _A_, _B_, _C_, … are being re-created in a reverse reaction between _R_, _S_, …. This bi-directional character of the process is indicated by the double-head arrow in the reaction equation. The rate of the reverse reaction can be written as

r' = k'·C R r·C S s…(3)

The net rate of the reaction is then equal to

r 0 = r - r' = k·C A a·C B b·C C c… - k'·C R r·C S s…(4)

The net reaction rate equals zero at equilibrium, _r 0 = 0_, thus

(C R r·C S s…)/(C A a·C B b·C C c…) = k/k' = K(5)

K is the reaction equilibrium constant, which depends on temperature. It can be calculated from thermodynamic functions of the system. Once known, the equilibrium constant allows for calculation of the concentrations of reaction products at equilibrium.

Reaction progress can be limited by reaction kinetics or by thermodynamic equilibrium. If the reaction rate constant (k) is low, the reaction, even if thermodynamically allowed, may take a long time to complete. Some reactions are so slow that their rates practically equal zero, and the processes could take years to complete. Fortunately, reaction rates may be increased by the use of catalysts.

On the other hand, if the reaction reaches its equilibrium, any further reaction progress is thermodynamically impossible. It is important to realize that catalysts have no effect on the reaction equilibrium. From the kinetics point of view, if a catalyst increases the forward reaction rate, Equation (2), it also increases, to the same degree, the reaction rate of the reverse reaction, Equation (3).

# 3.1: Basic Principles of Catalysis

![ ](https://bio.libretexts.org/@api/deki/files/11963/Kevin.jpg?origin=mt-web "Kevin Ahern, Indira Rajagopal, & Taralyn Tan")

A printable version of this section is here: [BiochemFFA\_4\_1.pdf](https://chem.libretexts.org/@api/deki/files/389303/BiochemFFA_4_1.pdf?revision=1 "BiochemFFA_4_1.pdf"). The entire textbook is available for free from the authors at [http://biochem.science.oregonstate.edu/content/biochemistry-free-and-easy](http://biochem.science.oregonstate.edu/content/biochemistry-free-and-easy "http://biochem.science.oregonstate.edu/content/biochemistry-free-and-easy")

If there is a magical component to life, an argument can surely be made for it being catalysis. Thanks to catalysis, reactions that can take hundreds of years to complete in the uncatalyzed “real world,” occur in seconds in the presence of a catalyst. Chemical catalysts, such as platinum, can speed reactions, but enzymes (which are simply super-catalysts with a “twist,” as we shall see) put chemical catalysts to shame (Figure 4.1). To understand enzymatic catalysis, it is necessary first to understand energy. Chemical reactions follow the universal trend of moving towards lower energy, but they often have a barrier in place that must be overcome. The secret to catalytic action is reducing the magnitude of that barrier.

![Biochemistry_Page_338_Image_0003.jpg](https://chem.libretexts.org/@api/deki/files/389296/Biochemistry_Page_338_Image_0003.jpg?revision=1&size=bestfit&width=668&height=234)

Before discussing enzymes, it is appropriate to pause and discuss an important concept relating to chemical/biochemical reactions. That concept is *equilibrium* and it is very often misunderstood. The “equi" part of the word relates to equal, as one might expect, but it does not relate to absolute concentrations. What happens when a biochemical reaction is at equilibrium is that the concentrations of reactants and products do not change over time. This does not mean that the reactions have stopped. Remember that reactions are reversible, so there is a forward reaction and a reverse reaction: if you had 8 molecules of A, and 4 of B at the beginning, and 2 molecules of A were converted to B, while 2 molecules of B were simultaneously converted back to A, the number of molecules of A and B remain unchanged, i.e., the reaction is at equilibrium. However, you will notice that this does not mean that there are equal numbers of A and B molecules.

## Concentration Matters

Contrary to the perceptions of many students, the concentrations of products and reactants at equilibrium are not equal to each other. The one exception to the previous statement occurs the ΔG°’ for a reaction is zero. Recall that ΔG° is Gibbs free energy change at standard state (1M concentrations of reactants and products), while ΔG°’ refers to the Gibbs free energy change for modified standard state (at pH 7, rather than 0, which would occur is proton concentration was 1M).

When ΔG°’ is zero,

\[ΔG = RT\ln \left(\dfrac{[\rm{Products}]}{[\rm{Reactants}]} \right)\]

Because ΔG itself is zero at equilibrium, then

\[[Products] = [Reactants].\]

This is the only circumstance where

\[[Products] = [Reactants]\]

at equilibrium. Reiterating, at equilibrium, the concentrations of reactant and product do not change over time. That is, for a reaction \(A \rightleftharpoons B [A]\) at time zero when equilibrium is reached, \([A]\_{T\_0}\), will be the same 5 minutes later (assuming A and B are chemically stable). Thus,

\[[A]\_{T\_0} = [A]\_{T+5}\]

Similarly,

\[[B]\_{T\_0} = [B]\_{T+5}\]

For that matter, at any amount of time X after equilibrium has been reached,

\[[A]\_{T\_0} = [A]\_{T+5} = [A]\_{T\_X}\]

and

\[[B]\_{T\_0} = [B]\_{T+5} = [B]\_{T\_X}\]

However, unless ΔG°’ = 0, it is wrong to say that at equilibrium, the concentration of products equals the concentration of reactants.

As we study biochemical reactions and reaction rates, it is important to remember that

1) reactions do not generally start at equilibrium;

2) all reactions move in the direction of equilibrium; and

3) reactions in cells behave just like those in test tubes - they do not begin at equilibrium, but they move towards it.

The reactions occurring in cells, though, are very dynamic and complex. In a test tube, they can be studied one at a time. In cells, the product of one reaction is often the substrate for another one. Reactions in cells are interconnected in this way, giving rise to what are called metabolic pathways. There are, in fact, thousands of different interconnected reactions going on continuously in cells.

Attempts to study a single reaction in a cell is daunting to say the least. For this reason, biochemists isolate enzymes from cells and study reactions individually. It is with this in mind that we begin our consideration of the phenomenon of catalysis by describing, first, the way in which enzymes work.

## Activation energy

Figure 4.2 schematically depicts the energy changes that occur during the progression of a simple reaction. In order for the reaction to proceed, an activation energy must be overcome in order for the reaction to occur.

![Biochemistry_Page_339_Image_0003.jpg](https://chem.libretexts.org/@api/deki/files/389297/Biochemistry_Page_339_Image_0003.jpg?revision=1&size=bestfit&width=612&height=363)

In Figure 4.3, the activation energy for a catalyzed reaction is overlaid. As you can see, the reactants start at the same energy level for both catalyzed and uncatalyzed reactions and that the products end at the same energy for both as well. The catalyzed reaction, however, has a lower energy of activation (dotted line) than the uncatalyzed reaction. This is the secret to catalysis - overall ΔG for a reaction does NOT change with catalysis, but the activation energy is lowered.

![Biochemistry_Page_340_Image_0002.jpg](https://chem.libretexts.org/@api/deki/files/389285/Biochemistry_Page_340_Image_0002.jpg?revision=1)  
*Figure 4.3 - Energy changes during the course of an uncatalyzed reaction (solid green line) and a catalyzed reaction (dotted green line). Image by Aleia Kim*

![Biochemistry_Page_340_Image_0002.jpg](https://chem.libretexts.org/@api/deki/files/389285/Biochemistry_Page_340_Image_0002.jpg?revision=1)

## Reversibility

The extent to which reactions will proceed forward is a function of the size of the energy difference between the product and reactant states. The lower the energy of the products compared to the reactants, the larger the percentage of molecules that will be present as products at equilibrium. It is worth noting that since an enzyme lowers the activation energy for a reaction that it can speed the reversal of a reaction just as it speeds a reaction in the forward direction. At equilibrium, of course, no change in concentration of reactants and products occurs. Thus, enzymes speed the time required to reach equilibrium, but do not affect the balance of products and reactants at equilibrium.

## Exceptions

The reversibility of enzymatic reactions is an important consideration for equilibrium, the measurement of enzyme kinetics, for Gibbs free energy, for metabolic pathways, and for physiology. There are some minor exceptions to the reversibility of reactions, though. They are related to the disappearance of a substrate or product of a reaction. Consider the first reaction below which is catalyzed by the enzyme carbonic anhydrase:

\[CO\_2 + H\_2O \rightleftharpoons H\_2CO\_3 \rightleftharpoons HCO\_3^- + H^+\]

In the forward direction, carbonic acid is produced from water and carbon dioxide. It can either remain intact in the solution or ionize to produce bicarbonate ion and a proton. In the reverse direction, water and carbon dioxide are produced. Carbon dioxide, of course, is a gas and can leave the solution and escape.

When reaction molecules are removed, as they would be if carbon dioxide escaped, the reaction is pulled in the direction of the molecule being lost and reversal cannot occur unless the missing molecule is replaced. In the second reaction occurring on the right, carbonic acid (H2CO3) is “removed” by ionization. This too would limit the reaction going back to carbon dioxide in water. This last type of “removal” is what occurs in metabolic pathways. In this case, the product of one reaction (carbonic acid) is the substrate for the next (formation of bicarbonate and a proton).

In the metabolic pathway of glycolysis, ten reactions are connected in this manner and reversing the process is much more complicated than if just one reaction was being considered.

## General mechanisms of action

As noted above, enzymatically catalyzed reactions are orders of magnitude faster than uncatalyzed and chemical-catalyzed reactions. The secret of their success lies in a fundamental difference in their mechanisms of action.

Every chemistry student has been taught that a catalyst speeds a reaction without being consumed by it. In other words, the catalyst ends up after a reaction just the way it started so it can catalyze other reactions, as well. Enzymes share this property, but in the middle, during the catalytic action, an enzyme is transiently changed. In fact, it is the ability of an enzyme to change that leads to its incredible efficiency as a catalyst.

## Changes

These changes may be subtle electronic ones, more significant covalent modifications, or structural changes arising from the flexibility inherent in enzymes, but not present in chemical catalysts. Flexibility allows movement and movement facilitates alteration of electronic environments necessary for catalysis. Enzymes are, thus, much more efficient than rigid chemical catalysts as a result of their abilities to facilitate the changes necessary to optimize the catalytic process.

## Substrate binding

Another important difference between the mechanism of action of an enzyme and a chemical catalyst is that an enzyme has binding sites that not only ‘grab’ the substrate (molecule involved in the reaction being catalyzed), but also place it in a position to be electronically induced to react, either within itself or with another substrate.

The enzyme itself may play a role in the electronic induction or the induction may occur as a result of substrates being placed in very close proximity to each other. Chemical catalysts have no such ability to bind substrates and are dependent upon them colliding in the right orientation at or near their surfaces.

## Active site

Reactions in an enzyme are catalyzed at a specific location within it known as the ‘active site’. Substrates bind at the active site and are oriented to provide access for the relevant portion of the molecule to the electronic environment of the enzyme where catalysis occurs.

## Enzyme flexibility

As mentioned earlier, a difference between an enzyme and a chemical catalyst is that an enzyme is flexible. Its slight changes in shape (often arising from the binding of the substrate itself) help to optimally position substrates for reaction after they bind.

![Biochemistry_Page_341_Image_0003.jpg](https://chem.libretexts.org/@api/deki/files/389288/Biochemistry_Page_341_Image_0003.jpg?revision=1&size=bestfit&width=759&height=525)

![Biochemistry_Page_342_Image_0003.jpg](https://chem.libretexts.org/@api/deki/files/389298/Biochemistry_Page_342_Image_0003.jpg?revision=1&size=bestfit&width=474&height=627)  
Figure 4.5 - Lysozyme with substrate binding site (blue), active site (red) and bound substrate (black). Wikipedia

![Biochemistry_Page_342_Image_0003.jpg](https://chem.libretexts.org/@api/deki/files/389298/Biochemistry_Page_342_Image_0003.jpg?revision=1&size=bestfit&width=474&height=627)

## Induced fit

These changes in shape are explained, in part, by Koshland’s Induced Fit Model of Catalysis (Figure 4.6), which illustrates that not only do enzymes change substrates, but that substrates also transiently change enzyme structure. At the end of the catalysis, the enzyme is returned to its original state. Koshland’s model is in contrast to the Fischer Lock and Key model, which says simply that an enzyme has a fixed shape that is perfectly matched for binding its substrate(s). Enzyme flexibility also is important for control of enzyme activity. Enzymes alternate between the T (tight) state, which is a lower activity state and the R (relaxed) state, which has greater activity.

![Biochemistry_Page_343_Image_0003.jpg](https://chem.libretexts.org/@api/deki/files/389302/Biochemistry_Page_343_Image_0003.jpg?revision=1&size=bestfit&width=474&height=511)

## Induced Fit

The Koshland Induced Fit model of catalysis postulates that enzymes are flexible and change shape on binding substrate. Changes in shape help to 1) aid binding of additional substrates in reactions involving more than one substrate and/or 2) facilitate formation of an electronic environment in the enzyme that favors catalysis. This model is in contrast to the Fischer Lock and Key Model of catalysis which considers enzymes as having pre-formed substrate binding sites.

## Ordered binding

The Koshland model is consistent with multi-substrate binding enzymes that exhibit ordered binding of substrates. For these systems, binding of the first substrate induces structural changes in the enzyme necessary for binding the second substrate.

There is considerable experimental evidence supporting the Koshland model. Hexokinase, for example, is one of many enzymes known to undergo significant structural alteration after binding of substrate. In this case, the two substrates are brought into very close proximity by the induced fit and catalysis is made possible as a result.

## Reaction types

Enzymes that catalyze reactions involving more than one substrate, such as

\[A + B \rightleftharpoons C + D\]

can act in two different ways. Enzymatic reactions can be of several types, as shown in Figure 4.7. In one mechanism, called sequential reactions, at some point in the reaction, both substrates will be bound to the enzyme. There are, in turn, two different ways in which this can occur - random and ordered.

*Figure 4.7 - Categories of enzymatic reactions*

| **Types of Reactions** |  |
| --- | --- |
| Single Substrate - Single Product | A ⇄ B |
| Single Substrate - Multiple Products | A ⇄ B + C |
| Multiple Substrates - Single Products | A + B ⇄ C |
| Multiple Substrates - Multiple Products | A + B ⇄ C + D |

Consider lactate dehydrogenase, which catalyzes the reaction below:

\[NADH + Pyruvate → Lactate + NAD^+\]

This enzyme requires that NADH must bind prior to the binding of pyruvate. As noted earlier, this is consistent with an induced fit model of catalysis. In this case, binding of the NADH changes the enzyme shape/environment so that pyruvate can bind and without binding of NADH, the substrate cannot access the pyruvate binding site. This type of multiple substrate reaction is called **sequential ordered binding**, because the binding of substrates must occur in the right order for the reaction to proceed.

## Random binding

A second mechanism of binding/catalysis is exhibited by creatine kinase which catalyzes the following reaction:

\[Creatine + ATP → \text{Creatine phosphate} + ADP\]

For this enzyme, substrates can bind to it in any order. Creatine kinase displays sequential random binding. It is worth mentioning that random binding is not inconsistent with Koshland’s induced fit model. Rather, random binding simply means that the enzyme’s induced fit doesn’t affect substrate binding sites and involves other parts of the enzyme. In summary, sequential binding can occur as ordered binding or as random binding.

## Double displacement reactions

Not all enzymes that catalyze multi-substrate reactions, though, bind A and B by the sequential mechanisms above. This other category of enzyme includes those that exhibit what are called “ping-pong” (or double displacement) mechanisms. In these enzymes, the enzyme functions as both a catalyst and a carrier of a group between individually bound substrates. Examples of this type of enzyme include the class of enzymes known as transaminases. A general form of the reactions catalyzed by these enzymes is shown in Figure 4.8.

![Biochemistry_Page_345_Image_0005.jpg](https://chem.libretexts.org/@api/deki/files/389287/Biochemistry_Page_345_Image_0005.jpg?revision=1&size=bestfit&width=236&height=431)

In reversible transaminase reactions, an oxygen and an amine are swapping between the molecules. It can be represented as follows (where N is the amine and O is the oxygen).

A=O + C=N ⇄ B-N + D=O

This reaction occurs not as one transfer reaction swapping the N and the O, but rather as a set of two half-reactions. In this case, the enzyme is a both donor and a carrier of the group being swapped. The first half-reaction goes as follows

A=O + Enz-N ⇄ B-N + Enz=O

Next a second half-reaction goes as

C-N + Enz=O ⇄ D=O + Enz=N

The sum of these half-reactions then is

A=O + C=N ⇄ B-N + D=O

Note that at no time did the enzyme bind both A and C simultaneously. It is also important to recognize that the enzyme existed in two states - Enz=O and Enz-N. The shuffling of the enzyme between these two states is what gives rise to the ping-pong name of this mechanism - it literally goes back and forth like a ping-pong ball in a table tennis match.

![Biochemistry_Page_346_Image_0002.jpg](https://chem.libretexts.org/@api/deki/files/389290/Biochemistry_Page_346_Image_0002.jpg?revision=1&size=bestfit&width=510&height=234)![Biochemistry_Page_346_Image_0003.jpg](https://chem.libretexts.org/@api/deki/files/389291/Biochemistry_Page_346_Image_0003.jpg?revision=1&size=bestfit&width=421&height=203)![Biochemistry_Page_346_Image_0004.jpg](https://chem.libretexts.org/@api/deki/files/389280/Biochemistry_Page_346_Image_0004.jpg?revision=1&size=bestfit&width=337&height=160)![Biochemistry_Page_347_Image_0002.jpg](https://chem.libretexts.org/@api/deki/files/389300/Biochemistry_Page_347_Image_0002.jpg?revision=1&size=bestfit&width=421&height=190)![Biochemistry_Page_347_Image_0003.jpg](https://chem.libretexts.org/@api/deki/files/389289/Biochemistry_Page_347_Image_0003.jpg?revision=1&size=bestfit&width=493&height=199)![Biochemistry_Page_347_Image_0004.jpg](https://chem.libretexts.org/@api/deki/files/389277/Biochemistry_Page_347_Image_0004.jpg?revision=1&size=bestfit&width=571&height=500)

## Enzyme kinetics

To understand how an enzyme enhances the rate of a reaction, we must understand enzyme kinetics. We present a model here proposed by Leonor Michaelis and Maud Menten. In order to understand the model, it is necessary to understand a few parameters.

First, we describe a reaction in simple terms proceeding as follows

E + S ⇄ ES -> E + P

where E is enzyme, S is substrate, and P is product. In this scheme, ES is the Enzyme-Substrate complex, which is simply the enzyme bound to its substrate.

We could define the ES state a bit further with

E + S ⇄ ES -> ES\* -> EP -> E + P

where ES\* is the activated state and EP is the enzyme-product complex before release of the product.

The first consideration we have is velocity. The velocity of a reaction is the rate of creation of product over time, measured as the concentration of product per time. The time is a critical consideration when measuring velocity. In a closed system (in which an enzyme operates), all reactions will advance towards equilibrium. Enzymatically catalyzed reactions are no different in the end result from non-enzymatic reactions, except that they get to equilibrium faster.

## Equilibrium

At equilibrium, the ratio of product to reactant does not change. That is a property of equilibrium. Since the system is closed, the concentration of product over time will not change. The velocity will thus be zero under these conditions and we will have learned nothing about the reaction if we wait too long to study it.

## Velocity

Consequently, in Michaelis-Menten kinetics, velocity is measured as initial velocity (Vo). This is accomplished by measuring the rate of formation of product early in the reaction before equilibrium is established and under these conditions, there is very little if any of the reverse reaction occurring.

The other two assumptions are related. First, we use conditions where there is much more substrate than enzyme. This makes sense. If the substrate is not in great excess, then the enzyme’s conversion of substrate to product will occur much faster than the enzyme can bind substrate.

## Waiting for substrate

Thus, the enzyme would “wait” for substrate to bind if there were not sufficient amounts of it to bind to the enzyme in a timely fashion (when substrate concentration is low). This would not give an accurate measure of velocity, since the enzyme would be inactive a good deal of the time. Because of this, we assume saturation of the enzyme with substrate will give a maximal velocity of the reaction.

## Steady state

![Biochemistry_Page_348_Image_0002.jpg](https://chem.libretexts.org/@api/deki/files/389301/Biochemistry_Page_348_Image_0002.jpg?revision=1&size=bestfit&width=358&height=287)![Biochemistry_Page_348_Image_0003.jpg](https://chem.libretexts.org/@api/deki/files/389278/Biochemistry_Page_348_Image_0003.jpg?revision=1&size=bestfit&width=682&height=529)

![Biochemistry_Page_349_Image_0002.jpg](https://chem.libretexts.org/@api/deki/files/389292/Biochemistry_Page_349_Image_0002.jpg?revision=1&size=bestfit&width=516&height=399)  
Figure 4.17 - Steady state versus non-steady state conditions

![Biochemistry_Page_349_Image_0002.jpg](https://chem.libretexts.org/@api/deki/files/389292/Biochemistry_Page_349_Image_0002.jpg?revision=1&size=bestfit&width=516&height=399)

Last, the high concentration of substrate combined with measuring initial conditions results in studying reactions that are under so-called steady state conditions (Figure 4.17). When steady state occurs, the concentration of the ES complex over time is not significantly changing during the period of analysis.

Reiterating, the three assumptions for Michaelis-Menten kinetics are

## Experimental considerations

Now we turn our attention to how studies of the kinetic properties of an enzyme are conducted. To perform an analysis, one would do the following experiment - 20 different tubes would be set up with enzyme buffer (to keep the enzyme stable), the same amount of enzyme, and then a different amount of substrate in each tube, ranging from tiny amounts in the first tubes to very large amounts in the last tubes. The reaction would be allowed to proceed for a fixed, short amount of time and then the reaction would be stopped and the amount of product contained in each tube would be determined.

The initial velocity (V0) of the reaction then would be the concentration of product found in each tube divided by the time that the reaction was allowed to run. Data from the experiment would be plotted on a graph using initial velocity (V0) on the Y-axis and the concentration of substrate on the X-axis, each tube, of course having a unique reaction velocity corresponding to a unique substrate concentration.

For an enzyme following Michaelis-Menten kinetics, a curve like that shown in Figure 4.18 or 4.19 would result. At low concentration of substrate, it is limiting and the enzyme converts it into product as soon as it can bind it. Consequently, at low concentrations of substrate, the rate of increase of [P] is almost linear with [S] (Figure 4.19).

![Biochemistry_Page_350_Image_0002.jpg](https://chem.libretexts.org/@api/deki/files/389276/Biochemistry_Page_350_Image_0002.jpg?revision=1&size=bestfit&width=229&height=226)

![Biochemistry_Page_351_Image_0004.jpg](https://chem.libretexts.org/@api/deki/files/389272/Biochemistry_Page_351_Image_0004.jpg?revision=1&size=bestfit&width=462&height=361)  
Figure 4.19 - Linear relationship between [P] and [S] at low [S]

![Biochemistry_Page_351_Image_0004.jpg](https://chem.libretexts.org/@api/deki/files/389272/Biochemistry_Page_351_Image_0004.jpg?revision=1&size=bestfit&width=462&height=361)

## Non-linear increase

As the substrate concentration increases, however, the velocity of the reaction in tubes with higher substrate concentration ceases to increase linearly and instead begins to flatten out, indicating that as the substrate concentration gets higher and higher, the enzyme has a harder time keeping up to convert the substrate to product.

## Saturation

Not surprisingly, when the enzyme becomes completely saturated with substrate, it will not have to wait for substrate to diffuse to it and will therefore be operating at maximum velocity.

For an enzyme following Michaelis-Menten kinetics will have its velocity (v) at any given substrate concentration given by the following equation:

![Biochemistry_Page_351_Image_0002.jpg](https://chem.libretexts.org/@api/deki/files/389273/Biochemistry_Page_351_Image_0002.jpg?revision=1&size=bestfit&width=183&height=47)

![Biochemistry_Page_351_Image_0002.jpg](https://chem.libretexts.org/@api/deki/files/389273/Biochemistry_Page_351_Image_0002.jpg?revision=1&size=bestfit&width=183&height=47)

## Vmax

Two terms in the equation above require explanation. The first is Vmax. It refers to the maximum velocity of an enzymatic reaction. Maximum velocity for a reaction occurs when an enzyme is saturated with substrate. Saturation is important because it means (per the assumption above) that none of the enzyme molecules are “waiting” for substrate after a product is released. Saturation ensures that another substrate is always instantly available. The unit of Vmax is concentration of product per time = [P]/time.

On a plot of initial velocity versus substrate concentration (V0 vs. [S]), Vmax is the value on the Y axis that the curve asymptotically approaches (dotted line in Figure 4.20). It should be noted that the value of Vmax depends on the amount of enzyme used in a reaction. If you double the amount of enzyme used, you will double the Vmax. If one wanted to compare the velocities of two different enzymes, it would be necessary to use the same amounts of enzyme in the reaction each one catalyzes.

![Biochemistry_Page_352_Image_0003.jpg](https://chem.libretexts.org/@api/deki/files/389286/Biochemistry_Page_352_Image_0003.jpg?revision=1&size=bestfit&width=384&height=347)

## Km

The second term is Km (also known as Ks). Referred to as the Michaelis constant, Km is the substrate concentration that causes the enzyme to work at half of maximum velocity (Vmax/2). What it measures, in simple terms, is the affinity an enzyme has for its substrate. The value of Km is inversely related to the affinity of the enzyme for its substrate. Enzymes with a high Km value will have a lower affinity for their substrate (will take more substrate to get to Vmax/2) whereas those with a low Km will have high affinity and take less substrate to get to Vmax/2. The unit of Km is concentration.

Affinities of enzymes for substrates vary considerably, so knowing Km helps us to understand how well an enzyme is suited to the substrate being used. Measurement of Km depends on the measurement of Vmax.

## Common mistake

A common mistake students make in describing Vmax is saying that Km = Vmax /2. This is, of course, not true. Km is a substrate concentration and is the amount of substrate it takes for an enzyme to reach Vmax /2. On the other hand Vmax /2 is a velocity and a velocity certainly cannot equal a concentration.

## Kcat

It is desirable to have a measure of velocity that is independent of enzyme concentration. Remember that Vmax depended on the amount of enzyme used. For this, we use the Kcat, also known as the turnover number. Kcat is a number that requires one to first determine Vmax for an enzyme and then divide the Vmax by the concentration of enzyme used to determine Vmax. Thus,

Kcat = V

enriched_points.expanded_content: Catalytic Reaction Engineering integrates chemical kinetics, thermodynamics, and transport phenomena, essential for understanding how catalysts influence reaction rates and selectivity in industrial processes.

Kinetics focuses on reaction rates while thermodynamics assesses the feasibility and equilibrium of reactions. Together, they establish performance metrics for catalyst effectiveness and reactor design.

Various reactor configurations, such as packed bed and fluidized bed reactors, utilize catalysts for applications in petrochemicals, pharmaceuticals, and renewable energy, demonstrating their industrial significance.

Ongoing research aims to discover new catalysts and optimize existing processes, driving advancements in sustainable technologies and addressing global challenges in energy efficiency and waste reduction.
